Sadashiv Rao Bhau

[Maratha History](https://sadashree.substack.com/s/maratha-history/?utm_source=substack&utm_medium=menu)

# Sadashiv Rao Bhau

### The 3rd Battle of Panipat

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Jan 14, 2025

Share

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!T0UR!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Ffabb7351-9641-4566-a2e5-ee3333002353_158x256.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!T0UR!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Ffabb7351-9641-4566-a2e5-ee3333002353_158x256.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Sadashivrao Bhau’s legacy is a complex tapestry of triumph and tragedy. The rout at Panipat in 1761 was catastrophic, not just militarily but emotionally and symbolically. His decision to carry thousands of civilians on a military campaign, his failure to forge alliances with the Jats, Rajputs, and Sikhs, and his rigid tactical choices all contributed to the Maratha defeat.Yet, it’s reductive to remember him solely for that.

He was instrumental in expanding Maratha influence in the Deccan and Karnataka, capturing key cities like Bagalkot, Badami, and Basavapatna. His victory at the Battle of Udgir in 1760 against the Nizam of Hyderabad was a masterstroke, forcing the Nizam to cede vital territories.Bhau also modernized the Maratha army, introducing disciplined infantry and artillery units inspired by European warfare .

Nanasaheb Peshwa his cousin, had transformed Pune from a provincial town into a thriving capital. He developed new localities like Nana Peth, Shaniwar Peth, and Raviwar Peth. He built the Parvati temple and the Katraj reservoir, which supplied water to Shaniwarwada , a feat of engineering that still functions today.

Under his leadership, the Maratha Empire reached its zenith, stretching from the Indus to Bengal. Sadly the very men who elevated the empire to its peak were also the ones whose legacies were shadowed by its most painful fall.

**Early Days**

Bhau’s birth on August 4, 1730, near Pune marked the continuation of a formidable Maratha lineage. His father, Chimaji Appa, was a brilliant strategist who led the campaign against the Portuguese, culminating in the capture of Vasai Fort in 1739, liberating the Konkan coast from colonial rule. That victory wasn’t just military — it was symbolic, reclaiming sacred land and restoring pride to the Maratha heartland.

And then there was his uncle, the legendary **Baji Rao I**, who **never lost a battle** in his lifetime,expanded Maratha influence deep into **Malwa, Bundelkhand, Gujarat**, and even up to the **Khyber Pass**, challenging Mughal supremacy and his daring **Raid on Delhi in 1737** sent shockwaves through the Mughal court, asserting Maratha dominance in northern India.

After the death of his mother Rakhmabai when he was just a month old, and his father Chimaji Appa at age ten, Bhau was raised by his grandmother Radhabai and his aunt **Kashibai**, the widow of Baji Rao I.

His education under Ramchandra Baba Shenvi, a brilliant statesman and administrator, was pivotal. Shenvi was no mere tutor, he was a strategist who later served as Diwan to Ranoji Scindia and played a role in temple restorations like Mahakaal Jyotirlinga in Ujjain.

Under his guidance, Bhau learned the intricacies of governance, diplomacy, and military logistics, skills that would later define his campaigns in Karnataka and against the Nizam.

**Deccan Campaign**

On December 5, 1746, Bhau departed Pune with trusted advisors Mahadoba Purandare and Sakharam Bapu, taking up the challenge where Babuji Naik and Fateh Singh Bhonsle had faltered. His first triumph came at Ajra, south of Kolhapur, where he defeated the Nawab of Savnur and captured the fort of Bahadur Bhenda. Bhau levied chauth and annexed 36 Parganas, expanding Maratha influence deep into Karnataka.

Bhau’s campaign laid the groundwork for future conquests in **Kittur, Gokak, Bagalkot, Badami, Basavapatna**, and more. Yamaji Shivdev, once a rebel, was subdued by Bhau’s strategic acumen and later appointed Diwan to Peshwa Balaji Baji Rao, Bhau’s own cousin.

In February 1760, Bhau led the Maratha forces to a decisive victory over the Nizam of Hyderabad at Udgir, Maharashtra. The Nizam was forced to surrender territories worth 60 lakhs, including Ahmadnagar, Daulatabad, Bijapur, and Burhanpur.

Bhau had now become the undisputed master of the Deccan, having already conquered Karnataka and now humbling the Nizam.Bhau’s campaign trail reads like a map of conquest, from **Ajra** to **Udgir**, each victory stitched a new thread into the Maratha tapestry.

**Advent of Ahmed Shah Abdali**

Ahmed Shah Abdali, the Afghan emperor, had begun his march toward Delhi, forging alliances with the Nawabs of Awadh, Rohillkhand, and Rajput rulers of Jodhpur and Amber. The Marathas were jolted by the death of Dattaji Scindia at the Battle of Burari Ghat on January 9, 1760, where he was ambushed and killed by Afghan-Rohilla forces near Delhi. With Delhi vulnerable and Abdali’s coalition swelling, the crisis demanded swift action.

Bhau, then basking in his victory over the Nizam at Udgir, was recalled to Partur by the Peshwa. Despite his brilliance in Deccan campaigns, Bhau was unfamiliar with the political terrain of North India, and lacked the diplomatic finesse needed to forge alliances with Rajputs, Jats, and Sikhs. His appointment as commander of the northern campaign was bold but hasty, and it would soon become clear that the stakes were far higher than anticipated.

In the Deccan, Bhau thrived , he knew the terrain, the politics, and the pulse of the people. But the North was a different entitty, you had the Rajputs, Jats, Sikhs each with their own histories, rivalries, and expectations. Bhau’s lack of familiarity with these dynamics meant he couldn’t forge the alliances that were crucial for facing Abdali’s coalition.

Unlike seasoned diplomats like Malharrao Holkar, Bhau struggled with negotiation and coalition-building. His failure to win over leaders like Suraj Mal (Jat), Rajput rulers of Jaipur and Jodhpur, and the Sikh misls left the Marathas isolated.

The Maratha tendency to interfere in succession disputes and impose authority didn’t help either ,it bred mistrust among potential allies.

Bhau’s strength lay in military innovation ,he modernized the Maratha army with infantry and artillery, inspired by European tactics. But war in the North wasn’t just about firepower ,it was about alliances, timing, and trust. And in that arena, Bhau was tragically outmatched.

Bhau’s decision to carry **100,000 civilians**, including **family members and pilgrims**, on a military expedition was not just unconventional, it was a disaster. Many of these non-combatants hoped to visit Hindu holy sites in the North, believing they’d be safe under Maratha protection.

But in reality, they became a logistical nightmare, the army had to divert resources to feed, shelter, and protect them. Supplies dwindled, mobility slowed, and strategic flexibility was lost. The Maratha force, once swift and agile, became encumbered and exposed.

In a campaign that demanded speed, alliances, and adaptability, Bhau’s decision added emotional weight and operational drag. It also alienated potential allies, Rajputs and Jats saw this as a sign of arrogance or recklessness. The civilians, tragically, were caught in the crossfire at Panipat, where the Maratha defeat turned into a massacre.

Traditionally, the Marathas relied on light cavalry and hit-and-run tactics, which worked brilliantly in the rugged terrains of the Deccan.

But Bhau, anticipating open-field warfare in the North, especially against Abdali’s disciplined Afghan forces, pivoted to European-style infantry and artillery. He brought in Ibrahim Khan Gardi, a master of artillery, and formed a corps of 10,000 infantry and 50 artillery pieces, aiming for a more structured, firepower-heavy approach.

However veterans like Malharrao Holkar were skeptical. They felt the army lacked the training, cohesion, and experience to execute these new tactics effectively. The integration of artillery and infantry clashed with the fluid cavalry maneuvers Maratha commanders were used to. This internal discord weakened unity ,a fatal flaw when facing a coalition as formidable as Abdali’s.

**Failure with Jats**

Suraj Mal, in particular, was wary of Bhau’s centralized command and refused to commit his forces , a decision that proved costly for the Marathas. He controlled the grain-rich belt around Delhi,, which was crucial for sustaining Bhau’s massive army and the 100,000 non-combatants he brought along.

Bhau’s dismissive attitude and refusal to accommodate Suraj Mal’s strategic suggestion, like fortifying Agra instead of open-field warfare, alienated the Jats.This led to a crippling shortage of supplies, forcing the Marathas into a siege-like situation before the battle even began.

**Failure with Sikhs**

The Sikhs, though once cooperative during earlier campaigns, were cautious of Maratha ambitions and chose neutrality. The **Sikhs**, hardened by decades of guerrilla warfare against Afghan incursions, offered to support Bhau.But Bhau, perhaps underestimating their military value or wary of sharing command, **rejected their aid**. This was a **strategic blunder** ,the Sikhs knew Abdali’s tactics intimately and could have provided both manpower and intelligence.

Post-battle, it was the **Sikhs who rescued thousands of Maratha women and children,**from Afghan captivity, attacking Abdali’s retreating forces and freeing the captives.Their valor in the aftermath only underscores how **crucial their alliance could have been** during the battle itself.

Bhau’s centralized vision clashed with the decentralized reality of northern India’s politics. The Marathas marched with might, but without meaningful allies, they stood isolated.

**Prelude to Panipat**

Bhau’s forces, led by Malharrao Holkar, Jankoji Scindia,, and others, launched a coordinated assault on Delhi, culminating in aMaratha victory on July 23, 1760. The Red Fort was bombarded, and after intense fighting, Yakub Ali Khan, Abdali’s general, surrendered.

Bhau appointed Naro Shankar as the new subahdar, signaling Maratha control ,but it was a hollow triumph.Local chieftains and nobles in and around Delhi remained aloof, wary of Maratha intentions and past interference in regional politics.

Bhau then marched north and executed a lightning assault on the fort of Kunjipura, near Karnal. Using his newly formed artillery and infantry corps, the fort was taken swift, it cut off Abdali’s supply lines and boosted Maratha morale, but also provoked Abdali into a rapid counter-march across the Yamuna.

Durrani was forced to flee from Kunjipura, as his entire garrison was massacred by the Marathas. It was a comprehensive victory for the Marathas over the Afghans at Kunjipura, some of Abdali’s best generals were killed. Once again presence of a large number of civilians in the Maratha contingent meant the supplies at Kunjipura were exhausted fast.

Despite these battlefield successes, Bhau’s campaign was strategically brittle, no alliances, no local support, and a massive logistical burden.The victories at Delhi and Kunjipura were isolated flashes of brilliance, not part of a cohesive northern strategy.

In a bold move,Abdali crossed the flooded Yamuna at Baghpat on a stormy October night.Despite losing over 200 soldiers to the river, he managed to bring his entire army across in two nights , a masterstroke that stunned the Marathas.This maneuver placed Abdali behind Bhau’s forces, effectively cutting off their retreat to Delhi and the Deccan.

The Marathas, realizing the threat, tried to block Abdali’s return route to Afghanistan, leading to a fierce engagement near Sonepat. Though the Afghans suffered around 1,000 casualties, they managed to repel the Marathas and encircle their forces, severing vital supply lines.This left Bhau’s army stranded and starving, with no reinforcements and dwindling morale.

Abdali’s encirclement turned Panipat into a siege,, not just a battlefield. The Marathas, burdened by non-combatants and logistical strain, were now trapped, facing winter, hunger, and attrition.By late November, Abdali had completely severed Maratha access to Delhi, trapping Bhau’s forces in Panipat.

The Afghans, with superior mobility and local support, cut off all food supplies, leaving over 250,000 people, including soldiers, families, and pilgrims,in dire straits.Cattle died en masse, grain ran out, and starvation began to claim lives. The once proud Maratha camp became a landscape of suffering.

Bhau’s army was isolated, with no reinforcements, no allies, and no escape route.Abdali’s siege tactics were ruthless and effective ,he turned the battlefield into a prison, slowly tightening the noose.

With morale shattered and starvation rampant,Maratha chiefs pleaded with Bhau to allow them to die fighting rather than waste away.

**Battle of Panipat**

On January 14, Bhau gave the order ,the Marathas marched out at dawn, determined to break the siege and reclaim their fate.At the dawn of Makar Sankranti, as the sun rose over Panipat,Sadashiv Rao Bhau led the Maratha forces in a full-scale assault, drums echoing across the plains.

The Gardi infantry, opened with precision artillery fire, followed by a cavalry charge that tore through Afghan flanks. By 2 PM, the Marathas had broken multiple Afghan lines, and Abdali’s forces began to retreat, stunned by the ferocity and coordination of Bhau’s attack.

Bhau didn’t just command ,he fought on horseback, rallying troops with his presence.His nephew Vishwas Rao, the heir to the Peshwa, also fought beside him, adding emotional weight to the charge. Afghan accounts describe the Maratha assault as so fierce that many soldiers fled, believing the battle was lost.

Just as the Marathas were pressing their advantage,Vishwas Rao, the Peshwa’s son and Bhau’s nephew, was struck , likely by astray musket or jamburak shot.His death sent shockwaves through the Maratha ranks.

He was not just a commander but a symbol of continuity and morale.Bhau, deeply attached to Vishwas Rao, was reportedly devastated, and the command structure began to unravel.

Sensing the disarray, Abdali launched a massive counteroffensive with 10,000 fresh troops, including elite cavalry and camel-mounted artillery.The Maratha forces were encircled, their flanks collapsed, and panic spread.Ibrahim Khan Gardi, who had held the center valiantly with his artillery, was overwhelmed. Jankoji Scindia was captured, and Bhau himself was surrounded from all ends.

Malharrao Holkar, seeing the tide turn and perhaps hoping to preserve some forces,retreated from the battlefield with thousands of troops.His departure is still debated, some view it as pragmatic, others as abandonment.The retreat left Bhau and the core Maratha leadership isolated and doomed.

In a moment of raw courage, he dismounted from his elephant , mounted a horse, and charged into the thick of battle, sword drawn, heart heavy.His empty howdah, visible to the Maratha ranks, sparked panic, many believed Bhau had fallen, and morale crumbled further.

Yet Bhau fought on, alongside Ibrahim Khan Gardi, whose disciplined infantry held the center till the very end. Surrounded, outnumbered, and betrayed by fate, Bhau refused to retreat, choosing to die with honor rather than surrender.

His body was found days later among the slain, a grim testament to his final charge.The battlefield bore witness to a man who embodied duty, sacrifice, and defiance, even when all hope had fled.
